info.topictitle: B2.3.4 Differences between totipotent, pluripotent and multipotent stem cells

markdown:
## Differences Between Totipotent, Pluripotent, and Multipotent Stem Cells


1. **Stem cells** are undifferentiated cells with the ability to **divide indefinitely** and **differentiate** into specialized cell types. 
2. They play a crucial role in **growth, development, and tissue repair**.
3. **Three main types of stem cells** are classified based on their potential to differentiate into different cell types:
  1. **Totipotent**
  2. **Pluripotent**
  3. **Multipotent**

![Stem cells](https://cdn.sanity.io/images/wla69xp2/production/14e9cc0ee13d0c87ec45585ceb7966b3496fc9b5-5288x4401.png)

## Totipotent Stem Cells: The All-Powerful Builders


<definition term="Totipotent Stem Cells">These are the most **potent** type of stem cells, meaning they have the ability to **differentiate** into **any cell type** in the body, as well as **extra-embryonic tissues** (such as the placenta). They can form an entire **organism**.</definition>

### Location


1. Found in the **early stages of embryonic development**. 
2. The first few divisions of the fertilized egg (zygote) produce **totipotent cells**.

### Function


1. Totipotent stem cells are capable of forming **all cell types** necessary for the development of an organism, including both **somatic cells** (cells that make up the body) and **germ cells** (cells involved in reproduction). 
2. They can also form the **extra-embryonic tissues**, like the placenta, required for supporting the developing fetus.

<callout type="example">* The **zygote** (fertilized egg) and the **early blastomeres** (cells formed after the first few divisions of the zygote) are **totipotent**. 
* These **cells can give rise to all the necessary tissues,** including the placenta, to support the embryo.</callout>

<callout type="analogy">Totipotent cells are like a **blank canvas, **they have the potential to become **any masterpiece** (any type of cell, tissue, or organ), including the **frame** (extra-embryonic tissues) that holds the artwork together.</callout>

## Pluripotent Stem Cells: The Specialists in Training


<definition term="Pluripotent stem cells">**Pluripotent stem cells** can differentiate into **any cell type** of the **embryo** (i.e., all **three germ layers - **ectoderm, mesoderm, and endoderm) but cannot form **extra-embryonic tissues** (like the placenta).</definition>

### Location


Found in **early embryos** (specifically in the **inner cell mass** of the blastocyst).

### Function


1. Pluripotent stem cells can differentiate into **any cell type** within the body, but they **cannot form extra-embryonic tissues** like the placenta. 
2. These cells are important for generating the **entire body** but not the structures that support the embryo outside the body.

<callout type="example">* **Embryonic stem cells** derived from the **inner cell mass** of the blastocyst are **pluripotent**. 
* These cells can become any type of cell found in the body (e.g., muscle, skin, nerve cells) but cannot give rise to extra-embryonic tissues such as the placenta.</callout>

<callout type="note">* Pluripotent stem cells are the type used in embryonic stem cell research and therapy. 
* They hold immense promise for treating diseases like Parkinson’s or regenerating damaged tissues.</callout>

<callout type="warning">* Don’t confuse pluripotent cells with totipotent cells. 
* While pluripotent cells can form all body tissues, they cannot create an entire organism or extra-embryonic tissues like the placenta.</callout>

## Multipotent Stem Cells: The Limited Specialists


<definition term="Multipotent stem cells">**Multipotent stem cells** are more **limited** than pluripotent cells. They can differentiate into **several** related cell types, but their potential is more restricted.</definition>

### Location


Found in **adult tissues** such as **bone marrow**, **skin**, and **intestinal lining**.

### Function


1. Multipotent stem cells are more **restricted** in what they can become. 
2. They can only differentiate into a **limited range of related cell types**. 
3. These stem cells are vital for maintaining and repairing the tissue in which they are located. 

<callout type="example">**Hematopoietic stem cells in the bone marrow** produce various **blood cells** (red blood cells, white blood cells, platelets), but they do not produce other cell types such as nerve cells.</callout>

<callout type="warning">* It’s important not to confuse multipotent stem cells with pluripotent stem cells. 
* Multipotent cells are far more limited in their potential and cannot differentiate into unrelated cell types.</callout>

![Types of stem cells](https://cdn.sanity.io/images/wla69xp2/production/76170ef9f655ee946c1074bd4b12f5331e6eba30-1336x1523.png)

## Comparing Totipotent, Pluripotent, and Multipotent Cells




| Type of Stem Cell | Location/Examples	 | Differentiation Potential |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Totipotent | Zygote (fertilized egg), early embryos | Can form the entire organism including the placenta |
| Pluripotent | Embryonic stem cells, blastocyst | Can form all cells of the three germ layers (ectoderm, mesoderm, endoderm) |
| Multipotent	 | Adult tissues (bone marrow, brain, etc.) | Can form cells within a specific tissue or organ lineage |



<callout type="tok">Should the potential to create a full organism (totipotency) carry greater moral weight than the ability to regenerate tissues (pluripotency)?</callout>

<callout type="self_review">* What is the key difference between totipotent and pluripotent stem cells?
* Give an example of a multipotent stem cell and the cell types it can differentiate into.
* Which type of stem cell is found in embryonic development and why is it considered pluripotent?</callout>
